heroes.name: Aamon
heroes.title: Duke of Shards
heroes.description: Aamon is a magic assassin whose real job starts after somebody else has damaged the target. His camouflage lets him choose the second entry, his shards punish runners, and his ultimate converts low health into a kill. Pick him when your team can start fights without him; lose with him when you ask him to start them.

heroes.name: Akai
heroes.title: Panda Warrior
heroes.description: Akai is a tank for teams that can kill one displaced target fast. He is not the safest generic roamer in a blind lobby. Pick him when the map gives you walls, your damage can follow the pin, and the enemy carry cannot reset the fight before Heavy Spin finishes.

heroes.name: Aldous
heroes.title: Soul Contractor
heroes.description: Aldous is a fighter built around delayed permission. He gives up early lane pressure so one stored punch can decide the side-lane pick or late objective fight, but only when his team can survive the map while he farms.

heroes.name: Alice
heroes.title: Queen of Blood
heroes.description: Alice is a drain-aura frontliner who wins fights by staying inside the enemy formation longer than their damage can hold. Pick her when the enemy team must stand near tanks or objectives to play the game. Avoid her when anti-heal, true damage, or repeated disengage can break the Blood Banquet loop before it pays you back.

heroes.name: Alpha
heroes.title: Blade of Enmity
heroes.description: Alpha is a bruiser for games where the enemy cannot end every fight in one clean burst. He is pickable when your team can start first or force clustered skirmishes, but he becomes a liability when anti-heal, long-range control, and mobile carries deny the repeated Beta hits that make him matter.

heroes.name: Alucard
heroes.title: Demon Hunter
heroes.description: Alucard is a lifesteal skirmisher, not a blind carry button. He wins when the enemy draft lets him chain skills into passive dashes without being pinned, and he looks ordinary when every entrance gets answered by suppression, knockups, or burst before his sustain starts working.

heroes.name: Angela
heroes.title: Bunnylove
heroes.description: Angela is a support who trades her own body on the map for the right to upgrade one teammate at the exact moment a fight breaks open. She is not a generic healer. Pick her when your draft has a diver worth attaching to, and avoid her when the enemy can cancel her channel or isolate her before Heartguard lands.

heroes.name: Argus
heroes.title: Dark Angel
heroes.description: Argus is an EXP lane fighter who wins by turning one committed target into health, tempo, and turret pressure. He is pickable when the enemy must fight him in melee range. He becomes a liability when the draft can kite, suppress, or waste his transformation without giving him something meaningful to hit.

heroes.name: Arlott
heroes.title: Lone Lancer
heroes.description: Arlott is a fighter whose value lives inside one condition: someone near him must be marked before he commits. He can look unfair when a CC-heavy team feeds him Vengeance resets, but he becomes ordinary when the draft gives him no setup, no clumped targets, and no time to farm Thunder Belt.

heroes.name: Atlas
heroes.title: Ocean Gladiator
heroes.description: Atlas is a roamer for teams that can turn one clean grab into a fight win. Patch 2.1.88 ([patch notes](/patch-notes)) raised his base HP and defenses to keep pace with the roaming attribute changes, so he eats the approach a little better before items come online. The pick still collapses if the enemy draft has purify, suppression, or too many instant exits.

heroes.name: Aulus
heroes.title: Warrior of Ferocity
heroes.description: Aulus is a bruiser for drafts that can hold an enemy in the fight long enough for Fighting Spirit to take over. His rework turned him away from old crit patience and toward contact, control-immunity timing, and repeated area pressure. Pick him when your team can start the first crack.

heroes.name: Aurora
heroes.title: Maiden of the Glacier
heroes.description: Aurora is a mid mage who turns Magic Power into longer lockdown, not just bigger damage. Pick her when the enemy has to walk into objectives and your team can protect a backline caster. Pick her into double assassin, Diggie, or long-range poke and the freeze becomes a liability instead of a win condition.

heroes.name: Badang
heroes.title: Tribal Warrior
heroes.description: Badang is a punishment fighter, not a blind engage hero. He wins when Fist Break turns terrain into a cage, Fist Crack keeps the target pinned, and attack-effect items make every punch matter. If the enemy draft can dash, purify, or suppress that channel on demand, the pick becomes a coin flip before the first wave even meets.

heroes.name: Balmond
heroes.title: Bloody Beast
heroes.description: Balmond is a low-maintenance fighter who wins when the game rewards staying in the middle of the fight longer than the enemy expected. Pick him for wave control, objective cleanup, and second-entry damage. Do not pick him when the enemy can repeatedly stop Cyclone Sweep or kite Soul Lock before the fight starts.

heroes.name: Bane
heroes.title: Frozen King
heroes.description: Bane is a side-lane pressure pick that turns skill casts into turret damage, then uses Sea Ink to stay on the map after trades other fighters would have to recall from. Pick him when your team can start fights without him and needs a lane that forces defenders away from Turtle, Lord, or mid pressure.

heroes.name: Barats
heroes.title: Dino Rider
heroes.description: Barats is a tank jungler who only looks simple when the fight starts on his terms. He is pickable when your team can play around a slow, stack-dependent frontliner and punish one swallowed target. He is a trap when the enemy draft can kite, cleanse, or shred a giant HP bar before Detona reaches the wall.

heroes.name: Baxia
heroes.title: Mystic Tortoise
heroes.description: Baxia is the tank you pick when the enemy draft plans to win by healing through your damage. He is not a blind engage roamer. He is a moving anti-sustain zone that needs terrain, follow-up damage, and a clear target list before the lobby starts.

heroes.name: Beatrix
heroes.title: Dawnbreak Soldier
heroes.description: Beatrix is four marksmen stuffed into one hero, and most of her losses come from players bringing the wrong one to a fight. Patch 2.1.67 ([patch notes](/patch-notes)) just turned her ultimate into a sustain tool by tying Spell Vamp to her Lifesteal. That changes the build, it does not change the draft rules: she still folds the moment the enemy has a hard answer to backline play.

heroes.name: Belerick
heroes.title: Guard of Nature
heroes.description: Belerick is a punishment tank, not a universal frontliner. Pick him when the enemy damage plan must hit your body many times before it reaches your carries. If the lobby is built around long-range poke, true damage, or instant backline dives, he becomes a slow tree walking into the wrong storm.

heroes.name: Benedetta
heroes.title: Shadow Ranger
heroes.description: Benedetta is not a swordsman. She is a flanking assassin whose entire kill condition lives inside a 2.5-second window. If your comp cannot deliver her to the enemy backline uninterrupted, do not lock her.

heroes.name: Brody
heroes.title: The Lone Star
heroes.description: Brody is the marksman you draft when every basic attack has to land harder than the other guy's, not faster. He trades attack speed for the heaviest per-hit number in the role, a ramping passive that rewards staying on a single target, and an ultimate that detonates harder the lower your enemies' health drops. No crit scaling, one stun, zero room for error.

heroes.name: Bruno
heroes.title: The Protector
heroes.description: Bruno is a gold laner who scales off crit consistency, not attack-speed volume. His passive converts every skill touch into free crit chance while taxing raw attack speed, which means his build path is narrower and his trade pattern is stricter than most marksmen. Pick him when your team can hold a front-to-back line and the match depends on one clean crit window after another.

heroes.name: Carmilla
heroes.title: Shadow of Twilight
heroes.description: Carmilla is not a crowd control support, she is a crowd control multiplier. Her ultimate links enemies together and shares 100% of every stun, 100% of every knockback, and 50% of every burst across the chain. The hero only works when the enemy draft gives you that chain to link.

heroes.name: Cecilion
heroes.title: Embrace of Night
heroes.description: Cecilion is not a mage in the burst sense. He is a mana-stacking countdown wrapped in a robe, a hero whose damage grows every time he casts a skill and whose usefulness is measured in how long the game goes. Draft him into the right shell and he wins fights at minute 20 that no other mage can win. Draft him into the wrong one and he never reaches minute 20.

heroes.name: Chang'e
heroes.title: Moon Palace Immortal
heroes.description: Chang'e is a siege mage who converts mana into map pressure. Her rabbit companion gives her a permanent shield, enhanced basics that chew through towers, and a Meteor Shower ultimate that turns jungle corridors into no-go zones. She is not a burst mage. She is a sustained-fire artillery piece that wins by forcing the enemy to choose between defending a split push and contesting an objective, then punishing whichever choice they make.

heroes.name: Chip
heroes.title: Phase Technician
heroes.description: Chip does not tank for your team in the normal sense. He is a portal with a health bar, and the health bar exists only so he can deliver the portal into the right position. His whole kit asks one question: can your team convert a teleport drop into a kill before the portal expires? If the answer is no, the pick is wasted regardless of how well you play the hero.

heroes.name: Chou
heroes.title: Kung Fu Boy
heroes.description: Chou is not a fighter in the traditional sense. He is a delivery service whose entire kit exists to grab one person and hand them to his team, dead. He works when the enemy has a specific target worth catching, and disappears when they do not.

heroes.name: Cici
heroes.title: Buoyant Performer
heroes.description: Cici is not the EXP pick you lock because you want a flashy duel. She is the fighter you choose when the enemy has to keep walking into you, because every extra second they stay attached to her yo-yo turns into a losing trade. The 2.1.88 patch slowed how fast Performer's Delight stacks decay between hits, so she now holds her speed and sustain bonus longer through repositions and short downtime windows.

heroes.name: Claude
heroes.title: Master Thief
heroes.description: Claude is a gold lane marksman who wins by preparing one controlled entry, not by standing still and out-ranging the enemy carry. Pick him when your team can start fights for him and when the enemy crowd control has visible cooldowns. If he has to invent every engage alone, Blazing Duet becomes a donation button.

heroes.name: Clint
heroes.title: West Justice
heroes.description: Clint is a gold-lane marksman who turns every spell cast into a problem for the enemy backline. He is built for players who want lane pressure that converts into mid-game burst, not for those who plan to passively scale. If your draft cannot station one body between Clint and the first diver, pick a different marksman.

heroes.name: Cyclops
heroes.title: Starsoul Magician
heroes.description: Cyclops is a mid laner whose damage ceiling is not a stat, it is a cooldown equation. Starlit Hourglass shaves 0.5 seconds off every cooldown per skill hit, which means a Cyclops who lands everything casts twice as often as one who whiffs. Played to that loop, he is a relentless poke engine that locks down carriers from across the screen. Played without it, he is a short-range immobile mage with no dash and a 120-second Flicker.

heroes.name: Diggie
heroes.title: Timekeeper
heroes.description: Diggie is not a support you pick because you like playing support. He is a veto button drafted into the game specifically to cancel the enemy team's ultimates, and if their draft has nothing worth cancelling, you have wasted a slot. The gap between his ban rate and his pick rate tells you everything: teams remove him from the pool far more often than they play him, because they would rather not watch their engage do nothing.

heroes.name: Dyrroth
heroes.title: Prince of the Abyss
heroes.description: Dyrroth is not a brawler. He is a tempo fighter who lives or dies on the short Abyss Enhanced window that opens when his Rage hits 50%, and patch 2.1.67 ([patch notes](/patch-notes)) now regenerates that Rage passively every second. The draft question is no longer "can he trade into Rage" but "can the enemy team deny his new S1-priority lane before he snowballs."

heroes.name: Edith
heroes.title: Ancient Guard
heroes.description: Edith is not a tank. She is a two-stage fighter who charges in mech form and discharges in marksman form, and every point of extra defense she stacks becomes magic power the second she ejects. Pick her when your draft has one reliable physical carry, a second initiator to split aggro, and the patience to wait for a lengthy ultimate to come back online.

heroes.name: Esmeralda
heroes.title: Astrologer
heroes.description: Esmeralda is not a shield caster. She is a shield thief. Her passive turns enemy shields into her own HP, which means her entire viability in a match is decided in the draft lobby before you touch the keyboard. Into the wrong lineup she is a mana-starved melee mage with no hard CC. Into the right one, the enemy frontline fights at half health from minute four.

heroes.name: Estes
heroes.title: Moon Elf King
heroes.description: Estes is not a healer. He is a link economy, a five-second tether that keeps one specific teammate alive while the rest of your team plays around an indestructible target. The pick wins when your draft has that target, and loses hard when it does not.

heroes.name: Eudora
heroes.title: Lightning Weaver
heroes.description: Eudora was the throwaway tutorial mage for nine years, then patch 2.1.47 quietly turned her into something else: a burst mage with real teamfight reach. Patch 2.1.88 ([patch notes](/patch-notes)) extended Superconductor from 3 seconds to 5 and made the S1 chain reduction scale with cooldown instead of a flat 1.5 seconds. She is no longer the joke pick, but she is also not free. The 5-second Superconductor window decides every fight she enters.

heroes.name: Fanny
heroes.title: Blade Dancer
heroes.description: Fanny does not walk. The moment she stops flying, she stops being Fanny. Every other assassin in the game approaches from bushes or fog. Fanny approaches from the air, and the mechanical demands of keeping her airborne are why most players who lock her end up feeding.

heroes.name: Faramis
heroes.title: Soul Binder
heroes.description: Faramis is a resurrection button wrapped in a mage kit. Every skill exists to get his team back into a fight they should have lost, and the player's only real job is casting Nether Realm at the exact moment the teamfight is about to collapse. Draft him when your team groups; bench him when they do not.

heroes.name: Floryn
heroes.title: Budding Hope
heroes.description: Floryn is not a hero, she is a buff that follows your carry. Lock her into a draft that already has a sustain-based damage-dealing core, then never let her get caught alone. The skill ceiling is low. The draft and positioning ceiling is everything.

heroes.name: Franco
heroes.title: Frozen Warrior
heroes.description: Franco is the only hero in Mobile Legends whose primary job is deciding where the enemy carry is allowed to stand. Iron Hook does not win teamfights by itself; it removes the enemy's right to hold a position, and that single removal is worth more than a kill most of the time.

heroes.name: Fredrinn
heroes.title: Rogue Appraiser
heroes.description: Fredrinn reads as a tank with damage scaling, but that framing hides the real mechanic. He is a delayed-reaction bruiser whose entire sustain engine runs on what the enemy team throws at him. If they refuse to commit damage, his Crystal Energy never stacks and the kit collapses into a mediocre taunt bot.

heroes.name: Freya
heroes.description: Freya after the November 2025 rework is a different hero. The 6 Sacred Orb system turned her from a "spam skills for attack speed" duelist into a resource manager: stock orbs before the fight, burn them during Valkyrie state, run dry and you are a melee bruiser with no escape. Patch 2.1.88 shortened the Valkyrie window from 12 to 10 seconds and raised the cooldown, so the cost of wasting orbs or mistiming the dive is higher than ever ([patch notes](/patch-notes)).

heroes.name: Gatotkaca
heroes.title: Mighty Legend
heroes.description: Gatotkaca is a tank that punishes tunneling. His kit is built around one bet: the enemy team will keep attacking him after he taunts them, and he will get stronger the more they do. Break that assumption and he is a slow, escape-less statue with a telegraphed ultimate.

heroes.name: Gloo
heroes.title: Swamp Spirits
heroes.description: Gloo is a lockdown tank that wins fights by turning an enemy carry into a piece of his own equipment. Two rounds of nerfs across patches 2.1.61 and 2.1.88 trimmed his passive damage reduction, his S1 goo chip, and the regen he banks during the attach window. The goo-reset loop and the attach still make him a contested roam or exp lane pick when the draft is right. When it isn't, he is one of the easiest tanks to kite into irrelevance.

heroes.name: Gord
heroes.title: Professor of the Mystics
heroes.description: Gord is a 2016 poke mage kept relevant in 2026 by being one of the cheapest magic-damage pressure tools in the game and one of the easiest to abuse. His entire kit hangs on a single stun skillshot, and every teamfight you win with him comes down to whether you land it from a spot the enemy assassin cannot reach.

heroes.name: Granger
heroes.title: Death Chanter
heroes.description: Granger is the only marksman whose cooldown reduction is also his damage stat. Every 1% of CDR he buys converts into 2 flat Physical Attack through Capriccio, which means his itemization looks nothing like Miya's or Claude's. Lock him when the draft wants a burst ADC, not a crit-scaling one.

heroes.name: Grock
heroes.title: Fortress Titan
heroes.description: Grock after the revamp is a different hero. The old version leaned on walls for defense and control immunity. The new version brings the wall with him, and his Physical Defense does the scaling. That changes how you draft him, how you initiate, and where on the map you are allowed to die.

heroes.name: Guinevere
heroes.title: Ms. Violet
heroes.description: Guinevere is a melee fighter who pays for her damage in mana she does not have and survivability she did not get. Lock her when your draft has a second source of crowd control to confirm her airborne window, and bench her when the enemy can interrupt one button.

heroes.name: Gusion
heroes.title: Holy Blade
heroes.description: Gusion is a one-second assassin. The window between "target alive" and "target dead" is shorter on Gusion than on any other hero in the game, and that speed is the entire point. Outside the combo window, he is a magic-damage assassin with one slow and one single-target blink.

heroes.name: Hanabi
heroes.title: Scarlet Flower
heroes.description: Hanabi is a draft-dependent gold laner whose real weapon is not raw range but the freedom to stand in a fight while everyone else is getting displaced. Pick her when the enemy wants to enter through one lane, one choke, or one front line, and skip her when your own draft cannot buy her eight clean minutes.

heroes.name: Hanzo
heroes.title: Akuma Ninja
heroes.description: Hanzo's rework flipped his risk model. The body you once had to hide is now invincible the moment his ultimate is active, and the fragile thing is the ghost you pilot and the Energy that keeps it alive. Pick him as a jungler who hunts the enemy carry through walls and walks away untouched, as long as you never feed Hanekage a free death.

heroes.name: Harith
heroes.title: Time Traveler
heroes.description: Harith is a hybrid dash-loop mage played in the Gold Lane, not a burst mage. He does not one-shot, he does not rotate cleanly for ganks, and he does almost nothing useful for the first six minutes. What he does do is turn a single skill cast into a two-minute cooldown cycle that an unprepared draft cannot peel off a backline.

heroes.name: Harley
heroes.title: Mage Genius
heroes.description: Harley was a fringe pick for years: flashy burst combo, but any competent tank could stand in front of the carry and eat Deadly Magic for them. The November 2025 patch changed the math. The ring of fire now passes through other heroes, which retires the single most-taught counterplay against him and quietly puts the Mage Genius back on the pick-off map.

heroes.name: Hayabusa
heroes.title: Crimson Shadow
heroes.description: Hayabusa is an isolation assassin. He is designed to mark one target, delete that one target, and leave before the fight starts. Every line of his kit rewards finding a solo enemy; every line punishes ulting into a group. If your draft cannot create pick-off windows, Hayabusa is the wrong jungler.

heroes.name: Helcurt
heroes.title: Shadowbringer
heroes.description: Helcurt is not a burst assassin who happens to silence. He is a silence assassin who happens to burst. The difference matters because every draft question, every fight, and every build slot routes through one test: can you press your abilities first, and can they press theirs? If the answer is yes, the kit falls apart. If the answer is no, he deletes the backline in 1.5 seconds.

heroes.name: Hilda
heroes.title: Power of Megalith
heroes.description: Hilda is not a tank in the Tigreal sense and not a damage roamer in the Popol sense. She is a bush-positioning mark builder whose ultimate turns into an execute only after the map gives her the free real estate to stack it. Pick her when the map has bushes that matter; bench her when the game happens on open river.

heroes.name: Hylos
heroes.title: Grand Warden
heroes.description: Hylos is not a tank in the classical engage sense. He is a mobile wall that trades Tigreal-style catch potential for the one thing no other tank can replicate: the ability to out-sustain a 5v1 and drag your squishies wherever they need to be. Pick him when your draft is built around that sustain, not around his ultimate.

heroes.name: Irithel
heroes.title: Jungle Heart
heroes.description: Irithel is a walking DPS turret with no brakes. She shoots while she moves, her ultimate cleaves grouped backlines, and her two-item spike ends games against tanks. After patch 2.1.67 ([patch notes](/patch-notes)) trimmed her base HP and physical defense while buffing her attack growth, she is faster to snowball and faster to fold. Peel her properly and she wins the match; let an assassin touch her and she dies in under a second.

heroes.name: Ixia
heroes.title: Arclight Outlaw
heroes.description: Ixia is not a gold laner you blind for comfort. She is a front-to-back draft check that punishes teams who stand in one lane, walk into chokes, or leave their backline exposed to a long cone of damage. Pick her when your team can hold space in front of her, and leave her alone when the game will be decided by dive pressure and forced repositioning.

heroes.name: Jawhead
heroes.title: Steel Sweetheart
heroes.description: Jawhead is not a fighter in the usual sense. He is a delivery service. His entire kit exists to pick one enemy out of a formation and hand them to his team with a bow on top, and every decision you make on him should answer the question "who am I about to deliver, and to whom."

heroes.name: Johnson
heroes.title: Wild Engine
heroes.description: Johnson is not a tank you pick to stand in front of your team. He is a long-range delivery vehicle, and after the 2.1.40 rework shifted his direct damage onto Physical Defense, every armor item you stack now doubles as a damage stat. The real draft question is never "is Johnson strong," it is "do we have a passenger worth delivering."

heroes.name: Joy
heroes.title: Pirate Fairy
heroes.description: Joy is the highest-skill assassin in the roster after Fanny, and she punishes you for picking her into the wrong draft harder than almost any other hero. The game does not let you brute-force her: every fight is a rhythm test, and missing a beat costs the kill.

heroes.name: Julian
heroes.title: Scarlet Raven
heroes.description: Julian is the hero you pick when your draft already has the crowd control and you want a jungler who turns a two-second window into three dead enemies. He is not a normal assassin. He is a combo-order puzzle wearing the jungle role tag, and he punishes every team that does not know which skill he still has ready.

heroes.name: Kadita
heroes.title: Ocean Goddess
heroes.description: Kadita is a burst assassin hiding inside a mage's role slot. Her entire kit exists to land one scripted combo on one squishy target, and her draft value collapses the moment the enemy team has a reliable answer. This guide covers when she is the right lock, how to execute the combo, and why most Kadita losses come from playing her like a normal mid laner.

heroes.name: Kagura
heroes.title: Onmyouji Master
heroes.description: Kagura is a displacement mage who happens to deal damage. Every other mid is priced on the burst they throw; Kagura is priced on the ground she controls with the Seimei Umbrella. The pick is only correct when your team specifically needs a pocket assassin who does not require peel to land her combo.

heroes.name: Kaja
heroes.title: Nazar King
heroes.description: Kaja is the single-target deletion button disguised as a roamer. His entire kit exists to feed one skill, Divine Judgment, and every other decision on this hero is whether he can land it on the right target, at the right angle, with his team in position to finish the job.

heroes.name: Kalea
heroes.title: Surging Wave
heroes.description: Kalea is not a teamfight tank. She is a single-target hook that only pays off when somebody else on your team is built to finish the hero she hands them. Lock her in the drafts where that follow-up exists; bench her in the drafts where it does not.

heroes.name: Karina
heroes.title: Shadow Blade
heroes.description: Karina is less an assassin than a kill-reset engine. Her ultimate cooldown resets on kill and her passive shaves cooldowns off every non-ultimate skill each time she hits a hero, which means the moment she catches the first low-HP target the rest of the enemy team is on borrowed time. That only happens if the draft actually gives her a first pick to catch.

heroes.name: Karrie
heroes.title: Lost Star
heroes.description: Karrie is the marksman you pick when the enemy draft wants to win by standing there and being difficult to kill. She loses value the moment the game becomes a range check or a dive race, so the right Karrie game starts in draft, not after the loading screen.

heroes.name: Khaleed
heroes.title: Desert Scimitar
heroes.description: Khaleed is sold in the hero pool as an EXP fighter, but the version that actually wins games in 2026 is a roamer carrying Thunder Belt. The Desert Power passive plus a Thunder Belt proc is the single cleanest true-damage tick a non-jungler can produce, and his ultimate is the longest CC-immune forced engage in the roam pool.

heroes.name: Khufra
heroes.title: Desert Tyrant
heroes.description: Khufra is not really a tank in the engage sense. He is an anti-mobility tool pretending to be a frontline, and the enemy draft decides whether he works. Against a dash-heavy carry he dismantles the game; against a poke comp he is an overpriced target dummy with slow cooldowns and nothing to do.

heroes.name: Kimmy
heroes.title: Hoverjet Outrider
heroes.description: Kimmy is not a marksman and she is not a mage. She is a walking turret that you build magic items on, whose basic attack is actually a skill shot, and whose attack speed stat quietly gets laundered into movement speed. Pick her only when the enemy cannot close on a kiter.

heroes.name: Lancelot
heroes.title: Blade of Roses
heroes.description: Lancelot does not fight. He phases through fights, dealing damage during the windows he physically cannot be touched. Time the invincibility frames correctly and he is one of the cleanest assassins in the game; time them wrong and he is a liability with a pretty dash animation.

heroes.name: Lapu-Lapu
heroes.title: Courageous Blade
heroes.description: Lapu-Lapu is not really a fighter. He is a 10-second transformation with eight minutes of setup, and his entire kit is a ramp toward that window. If your draft lets him reach it, he wins the game. If it doesn't, he throws boomerangs and loses.

heroes.name: Layla
heroes.title: Energy Gunner
heroes.description: Layla is a gold-lane siege cannon, not a mercy pick for drafts that already look fragile. She wins when your team can keep one layer of peel in front of her, the fight stays front-to-back, and the enemy has to walk through vision just to touch her.

heroes.name: Leomord
heroes.title: Sworn Sword
heroes.description: Leomord is a jungle fighter built around one temporary unfair state: mounted form with room to keep moving. He is not a blind bruiser and not a reliable first engager. Pick him when your draft can crack the fight open for him, then let the horse finish what your frontline started.

heroes.name: Lesley
heroes.title: Deadly Sniper
heroes.description: Lesley is not a front-to-back DPS check. She is a sniper who wins when the fight can be decided by two loaded shots before the enemy carry gets to play. If your draft cannot buy that time and that angle, she stops looking clever very quickly.

heroes.name: Ling
heroes.title: Cyan Finch
heroes.description: Ling is not a blind-pick assassin right now. He is a conditional jungle carry whose games are won before the first teamfight, when the draft either gives him safe blue buff cycles and open backline angles or denies both and turns him into a wall-hopping spectator.

heroes.name: Lolita
heroes.title: Steel Elf
heroes.description: Lolita is not a blind tank. She is a veto button for drafts that want to win through bullets, stars, cards, and long-range poke, and she feels ordinary the moment the enemy stops giving her something to reject. Pick her when one denied projectile window will decide the fight, then play the rest of the game around that stolen tempo.

heroes.name: Lukas
heroes.title: Beast of Light
heroes.description: Lukas is an EXP bruiser built around one short window: land Flash Step, stack two basics, then cash out Flash Combo before the target can reset the trade. He looks like a blind-pick brawler, but he is better treated as a reactive side-lane pick who wants one reachable backliner, one honest melee lane, and teamfights that last longer than the first burst.

heroes.name: Lunox
heroes.title: Twilight Goddess
heroes.description: Lunox is not a general-purpose mid mage. She is a short-range answer to drafts that must walk into her, and she only looks broken when the enemy gives her time to load the correct form before the fight starts.

heroes.name: Luo Yi
heroes.title: Yin-yang Geomancer
heroes.description: Luo Yi is a zone-control mage who wins by turning one caught target into two lost health bars. She is strongest when the draft gives her a front line to cast through and enemies who have to walk into choke points. She becomes a liability the moment the fight is all dashes and backline access.

heroes.name: Lylia
heroes.title: Little Witch
heroes.description: Lylia is a mid-lane tempo mage disguised as a reset button. She wins when she owns the river from level 1, forces enemies to walk through pre-seeded bombs, and rewinds before the punish lands. If your draft needs a patient backline scaler who waits for 15 minutes, lock a different mage.

heroes.name: Marcel
heroes.title: Split-Soul Artist
heroes.description: Marcel is a roaming support who wins by turning one chokepoint into a kill box. His value is conditional, not constant: he needs a doorway both teams have to fight through and a carry who can convert his freeze into damage. Pick him to pin a fight in place, never to start a dive.

heroes.name: Martis
heroes.title: Ashura King
heroes.description: Martis is not a hero you pick to start fights. He is a hero you pick to survive the first exchange and then collect the pieces. His second skill's control immunity is the entire identity: learn when to hold it and the rest of the kit clicks into place.

heroes.name: Masha
heroes.description: Masha is not a bruiser who survives fights by being hard to kill. She is a fighter who gets stronger the closer she is to death, and she has three chances to reach that state before she falls. Every decision you make on her should optimize for spending HP on offense, not preserving it.

heroes.name: Mathilda
heroes.title: Swift Plume
heroes.description: Mathilda is not a fighter who happens to support. She is a delivery system dressed as a hero. Her entire kit is designed to move other people where they need to be and then punish whoever they land on. Get that order of operations right, and she is one of the most disruptive roamers in the game. Get it wrong, and she is a very fast hero with nothing useful to do.

heroes.name: Melissa
heroes.title: Cursed Needle
heroes.description: Melissa after patch 2.1.67 ([patch notes](/patch-notes)) is not the hero she was. The S1 cooldown loop that defined her old playstyle is gone, replaced by a tighter kit where the blink chains straight into Muddles and the ultimate trades its passive defense for an on-cast shield. She is still a zone-tether DPS, but the way you farm the zone changed.

heroes.name: Minotaur
heroes.title: Son of Minos
heroes.description: Minotaur is the only roamer in MLBB whose kit gets more dangerous the harder the enemy team hits him. Once Minoan Fury activates his enraged state, his stomp covers more ground, his Motivation Roar heals every nearby teammate simultaneously, and the hybrid defense buffs and debuffs his passive applies both double. The gap between his normal and enraged state is the whole game.

heroes.name: Minsitthar
heroes.title: Courageous Warrior
heroes.description: Minsitthar is not a fighter who hunts enemies. He builds a prison, kicks the door shut, and lets his four Royal Guards do the math. Every part of his kit serves one purpose: forcing enemies to fight in a space they cannot escape from.

heroes.name: Miya
heroes.title: Moonlight Archer
heroes.description: Miya is a backline punishment test, not a comfort pick. She wins when your draft buys her one protected firing lane and loses when the enemy can reach her before her second item lands. The buttons are simple. The judgment is not.

heroes.name: Moskov
heroes.title: Spear of Quiescence
heroes.description: Moskov is a marksman who turns crowded fights into bad math. He is strongest when both teams are forced to stand in narrow spaces and weakest when the game becomes a dive test. Pick him for angle control and sustained chase, not because you want a blind-safe gold laner.

heroes.name: Nana
heroes.title: Feline Wizard
heroes.description: Nana is a CC battery disguised as a mage: the metric that matters is how often Molina transforms a key target, not how much damage she deals. She plays mid lane or roamer, and the two roles are not interchangeable. The patch 2.1.61 cut to Molina Smooch's cooldown has pushed her ceiling significantly higher in both positions.

heroes.name: Natalia
heroes.title: Bright Claw
heroes.description: Natalia is not a jungler you draft for raw damage output. She is a pressure system: enemies who ordinarily walk the map alone now need escorts, and carries who stand at the edge of vision start hugging their tower instead. That psychological weight disappears the moment a Franco hook or a Saber spin enters the picture.

heroes.name: Natan
heroes.title: Spacetime Walker
heroes.description: Natan is not a backline turret. He is a geometry marksman whose damage only looks unfair when the clone, the target, and your own firing line cut through the same fight. Pick him when your draft can pin enemies in place and protect one long damage window, and leave him alone when the game is about surviving repeated dives.

heroes.name: Nolan
heroes.title: Cosmic Wayfinder
heroes.description: Nolan is the assassin whose damage does not exist until two of his rifts touch. Pick him when your draft has peel for a short setup window and a squishy backline you can actually reach. Pick anything else when the enemy already locked Phoveus, Khufra, or Franco.

heroes.name: Novaria
heroes.title: Star Rebel
heroes.description: Novaria is not a burst mage. She is a siege weapon: slow to aim, devastating when the setup lands, and completely unplayable when the enemy refuses to stand still. She punishes slow, tanky teams from ranges most mages cannot reach, but she depends on specific conditions in the draft and in the fight to function at all.

heroes.name: Obsidia
heroes.title: Sovereign of Dark's End
heroes.description: Obsidia is not a standard late-game marksman. She is a trap you preload before the fight starts, then spring on the first carry who cannot break the tether. If you enter fights under-farmed, low on shards, or into the wrong frontline, she feels useless for reasons that draft usually told you in advance.

heroes.name: Odette
heroes.title: Swan Princess
heroes.description: Odette is not the mid laner you draft to win lane. She is the mid laner you draft to win a five-second window in the center of a teamfight, provided your team has already built a wall of CC and peel around her. Get that condition wrong and she becomes the easiest ult interrupt in the game.

heroes.name: Paquito
heroes.title: The Heavenly Fist
heroes.description: Paquito is not a comfort blind pick anymore. He is a tempo fighter who turns one clean stack cycle into lane control, river priority, and backline pressure, but only when the draft gives him targets he can actually stay on top of. If the enemy comp can deny dashes on reaction, the hero stops looking unfair very quickly.

heroes.name: Pharsa
heroes.title: Wings of Vengeance
heroes.description: Pharsa is not a comfort blind pick. She is a mid-lane artillery piece that turns first wave control into map pressure, then turns one safe angle into an objective zone the enemy cannot stand in. Pick her when your draft can keep divers honest and the fight will be played front to back, not as a five-angle brawl.

heroes.name: Phoveus
heroes.title: Chains of Sin
heroes.description: Phoveus is not a gimmick counter that only exists to swat dashes. He is an EXP bruiser who turns short-range chaos into repeatable mark loops, but only when the draft gives him targets he can actually stay attached to. Blind him into poke, long-range control, or lanes he cannot touch and the whole kit turns into a slow walk with a big weapon.

heroes.name: Popol and Kupa
heroes.title: Icefield Companions
heroes.description: Popol and Kupa is a marksman for players who want to decide where the fight can happen before it starts. He wins by shrinking the map with traps, tagging the first target that steps wrong, and cashing that mistake into tower pressure. Pick him when your draft wants controlled corridors, not when you need a free-scaling backline gunner.

heroes.name: Rafaela
heroes.title: Wings of Holiness
heroes.description: Rafaela is not a healer in the MMO sense. She is a tempo support who turns messy skirmishes into winnable ones by forcing movement speed, revealing ambushes, and buying one extra second for the right teammate. Pick her when that extra second wins the fight. Leave her alone when your draft needs a real frontliner or the enemy can erase her before the second cast lands.

heroes.name: Roger
heroes.title: Dire Wolf Hunter
heroes.description: Roger is a tempo jungler who wins by handing the same target from human form to wolf form without ever giving that target a reset. He is dangerous when your draft lets him hunt side angles and chase broken fights, and much worse when you ask him to be the first body through the front door.

heroes.name: Ruby
heroes.title: Little Red Hood
heroes.description: Ruby is not a pure all-in bruiser, but she hits harder now than her reputation suggests. She is a control fighter who wins by taking short trades, resetting with passive dashes, then dragging the next fight to a pace the enemy backline hates. Pick her when your team needs layered crowd control with a real damage threat behind it from EXP lane, not when the draft is begging for a solo carry.

heroes.name: Saber
heroes.title: Wandering Sword
heroes.description: Saber is not a jungler you blind because you want a clean 5v5. He is a draft weapon for deleting one hero on command, then making every objective fight feel unsafe for the enemy backline. If the draft gives him no clean target and no exit lane, pick someone else.

heroes.name: Selena
heroes.title: Abyssal Witch
heroes.description: Selena is a pick-comp mage wearing an assassin's body. She wins games by turning bushes into information and one long arrow into a numbers advantage, not by front-to-back damage. If your draft needs clean wave-clear scaling or a safe late-game backliner, pick someone else.

heroes.name: Silvanna
heroes.title: Imperial Knightess
heroes.description: Silvanna is a pick tool disguised as an EXP fighter. She wins when the draft gives her one high-value body to isolate and a second teammate close enough to punish the cage. She feels awful when you ask her to blind-frontline into peel, cleanse, or repeated displacement.

heroes.name: Sora
heroes.title: Shifting Cloud
heroes.description: Sora is an EXP pick whose value is decided by one question before the fight even starts: is this game asking for a finisher or a starter. He looks flexible enough to solve both on command, but the hero only feels oppressive when your draft, lane state, and first target all point toward the same answer.

heroes.name: Sun
heroes.title: Monkey King
heroes.description: Sun is a fighter who wins games by making the map wider than the enemy draft can comfortably cover. He is not a blind EXP answer and he is not your cleanest 5v5 starter. Pick him when the enemy lacks fast clone clear, and when your team can turn side-lane pressure into a real objective lead.

heroes.name: Suyou
heroes.title: Mask of the Immortal
heroes.description: Suyou is not a blind-pick assassin. He is a tempo jungler who turns one bad rotation into a dead backliner, then looks ordinary when draft asks him to front-to-back through peel, crowd control, and layered magic damage. Pick him for fast river fights and side-angle entries, not for slow siege.

heroes.name: Terizla
heroes.title: Executioner
heroes.description: Terizla is the EXP pick for drafts that need one body to start the fight, stay in the choke, and make the enemy carry hit through bad ground. He looks slow on paper, but the right game turns that slowness into control instead of a liability.

heroes.name: Thamuz
heroes.title: Lord Lava
heroes.description: Thamuz is an EXP bruiser who wins when the fight stays close enough for him to keep hitting. He is not a blind comfort pick and not a substitute for a real tank. Lock him when the enemy draft has to stand its ground, then build around one goal: keep contact long enough for the brawl to turn.

heroes.name: Tigreal
heroes.title: Warrior of Dawn
heroes.description: Tigreal is not a tank you blind-pick because you want a front line. He is a bush-controlled initiation test: if the enemy draft cannot reliably cancel or cleanse his engage, one clean combo decides the fight. If they can, he turns into a predictable wall with a long cooldown problem.

heroes.name: Uranus
heroes.title: Aesthereal Defender
heroes.description: Uranus is not an engage tank. He is a lane thief who turns wave tempo into map pressure, then dares the enemy team to waste real cooldowns chasing a target they still cannot finish. Pick him when your draft wants an EXP sponge that can cut, scout, and stall, not when it needs reliable lockdown.

heroes.name: Vale
heroes.title: Windtalker
heroes.description: Vale is a mid laner for players who want one clean fight to decide the map. He is strongest when your draft can start first and force enemies through narrow space, and he becomes a liability when the match turns into blind checking against dive.

heroes.name: Valentina
heroes.title: Prophetess of the Night
heroes.description: Valentina is not a blind-pick mage anymore. She is a draft tax on teams that bring one great ultimate and one fragile mid lane. When the enemy gives her both, she dictates the fight shape before Lord even spawns.

heroes.name: Valir
heroes.title: Son of Flames
heroes.description: Valir is not a burst mage with a self-peel button. He is a bouncer who turns one corridor of the fight into a bad place to exist. The pick works when the enemy must walk through him to play the game, and it fails when the enemy can ignore that corridor and land on his face anyway.

heroes.name: Vexana
heroes.title: Shimmer of Hope
heroes.description: Vexana is not a blind-pick mid. She is a punish mage that turns crowded fights and obvious movement into free damage. If your draft can start first or the map forces the enemy through narrow entrances, she feels oppressive; if you need her to create space alone, she looks slow and fake.

heroes.name: Wanwan
heroes.title: Agile Tiger
heroes.description: Wanwan is not a marksman who farms and wins by existing. She is a draft bet that says you can flank the enemy carry four times, hit every weakness, and cash the ultimate before a suppression lands. When that bet is wrong, her ult feels like a decoration on a squishy body.

heroes.name: X.Borg
heroes.title: Firaga Armor
heroes.description: X.Borg is an EXP laner who wins by making one trade last longer than the enemy planned for. He is not a blind comfort pick into every fighter lane. He is a frontliner with a fake second life, and the draft only feels easy when the enemy cannot break Firaga Armor before your fire turns the lane into dead space.

heroes.name: Xavier
heroes.title: Defier of Light
heroes.description: Xavier is not a generic poke mage. He is a map-control mid who wins when the fight starts on his line and loses when the enemy gets to start on theirs. Pick him for drafts that want to choke entrances, soften targets before the commit, and turn one clean objective setup into a fight the enemy enters at half health.

heroes.name: Yi Sun-shin
heroes.title: Paenlong Legend
heroes.description: Yi Sun-shin is a jungler for players who want one hero to scout, start, and finish the same fight. He looks like a free-carry assassin in highlights, but his real win condition is stricter: keep the weapon-switch rhythm intact, arrive after the first control lands, and make the map smaller for the enemy carries.

heroes.name: Yin
heroes.title: Martial Genius
heroes.description: Yin is a jungle fighter built around one question: can you find the right target and beat them alone in eight seconds? Everything in his kit feeds that question, and understanding its limits is what separates players who win with him from players who feed their own team.

heroes.name: Yu Zhong
heroes.title: Black Dragon
heroes.description: Yu Zhong wins fights that look like they're going against him. His passive converts sustained melee engagement into a regen engine: each 5-stack Sha Residue eruption deals HP-loss-based physical damage and restores a chunk of his own missing HP, which means every trade that drags on tilts in his favor. That loop is his identity, and managing it well is the entire game.

heroes.name: Yve
heroes.title: Astrowarden
heroes.description: Yve does not win teamfights by dealing the most damage. She wins them by turning a section of the map into a place the enemy cannot safely leave. Patch 2.1.61 ([patch notes](/patch-notes)) trimmed her base stats and slow percentages while significantly buffing her passive shield, sharpening the gap between players who understand her stack-and-geometry loop and those who treat her as a generic AoE mage.

heroes.name: Zetian
heroes.title: Celestial Empress
heroes.description: Zetian is not a mage you pick to solo-carry from mid. She is the answer to a specific draft problem: your team needs global reach, and the enemy composition punishes itself by clustering. Get the draft right and she wins teamfights from across the map. Get it wrong and she is a paper target with no escape.

heroes.name: Zhask
heroes.title: The King of Swarms
heroes.description: Zhask is a control mage for players who want fights to happen on their square, not the enemy's. Patch 2.1.61 made him easier to cycle and a little easier to position, but that did not change the real test: can your draft buy enough time and bodyguard space for the spawn to matter?

heroes.name: Zhuxin
heroes.title: Beacon of Spirits
heroes.description: Zhuxin is a zone-control mage who wins teamfights by stretching them into channeled 4-second windows that most mid laners physically cannot survive. She rewards patience, punishes impatient teammates, and collapses against any draft that can close 4 meters on her while she's holding a button down. Knowing which bucket your current game is in decides whether you should lock her.

heroes.name: Zilong
heroes.title: Spear of Dragon
heroes.description: Zilong is not a teamfighter. He is a 1v1 duelist with legs fast enough to farm two lanes at once, and the best Zilong games are the ones where his team never needed him to stand in the middle of a fight. After the patch 2.1.40 ([patch notes](/patch-notes)) buff that stapled flat damage onto his passive and let skills count toward Dragon Flurry stacks, his early and mid-game damage is real. What did not change is his defense: 25 base physical defense, cloth armor in every sense.
